misconceptionSummary: Students do not recognise which angles are vertically opposite and instead try to add them to 180° or 360° without using the equality rule.

visuals.ruleText: When two straight lines cross, the angles directly opposite each other are equal
visuals.subText: Vertically opposite angles are across the crossing point — not next to each other

steps.narration: When two straight lines intersect, they create four angles. The two angles that are directly across the crossing point from each other — not adjacent, but opposite — are equal. These are called vertically opposite angles.

lines.text: Two lines cross → four angles: a, b, c, d
a and c are vertically opposite → a = c
b and d are vertically opposite → b = d

steps.narration: Label the four angles a, b, c, d going around. Angle a is opposite angle c — they are equal. Angle b is opposite angle d — they are equal. Adjacent angles such as a and b are supplementary, adding to one hundred and eighty.

lines.text: Two lines cross. One angle is 70°.

steps.narration: One angle is seventy degrees. The vertically opposite angle is also seventy. The two adjacent angles are each one hundred and eighty minus seventy — one hundred and ten degrees. The four angles total three hundred and sixty.

visuals.label: Vertically opposite → equal.  Adjacent → supplementary (sum to 180°).

steps.narration: Seventy, one hundred and ten, seventy, one hundred and ten — totalling three hundred and sixty. Opposite pairs are equal; adjacent pairs add to one hundred and eighty.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: adding vertically opposite angles to 180° or 360° instead of recognising they are equal. Vertically opposite angles are across the crossing point — they are always equal, not supplementary.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Vertically opposite angles are equal — not supplementary. They sit across the crossing point, not next to each other. Adjacent angles (next to each other) are the ones that sum to 180°.
